pathophysiology_summary: Tinea corporis is a superficial dermatophyte infection of the glabrous (non-hair-bearing) skin, usually caused by Trichophyton, Microsporum or Epidermophyton species acquired from soil (geophilic), animals (zoophilic) or other people. The fungus invades and lives in the keratin of the stratum corneum. Its centrifugal outward growth produces the classic annular lesion: an advancing, raised, scaly, inflamed active border where the fungus is proliferating, with relative central clearing as the older central skin recovers. The intense pruritus reflects the local inflammatory response. In this child the vigorous scratching has produced secondary excoriation of the active borders — mechanical breaks in the skin with pinpoint bleeding and serosanguinous crusting — which is a self-inflicted complication, not spreading infection, but does create a portal for secondary bacterial colonisation/infection if it progresses. He is systemically well and afebrile, and the lesions lack the confluent hot induration of cellulitis or the thick honey-coloured crust of impetigo.

differential.evidence_basis: Enlarging annular red patches with a raised scaly advancing edge and central clearing in an outdoor-playing child are the textbook description of ringworm.

The carer reports bleeding and rapid spread and fears a spreading infection; excoriated weeping skin can become secondarily infected, so bacterial infection must be actively screened for and excluded.
Coin-shaped itchy plaques can mimic ringworm, but nummular eczema is uniformly inflamed and scaly throughout without the advancing scaly edge and central clearing.
Produces annular lesions, but classically smooth, non-scaly and asymptomatic rather than intensely itchy and scaly.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should hold ringworm as most likely while explicitly keeping secondary bacterial infection on the list to screen out — not anchoring on the carer's 'flesh-eating infection' fear.

differential.evidence_basis: Annular morphology with central clearing plus a systemically well, afebrile child whose lesion edges are not hot, indurated or honey-crusted — the bleeding is mechanical from scratching.
No fever, no confluent hot induration, no thick honey-coloured crust or pus — the negative red-flag screen makes an active bacterial infection unlikely at this point.
Remains a lower-probability mimic; distinguished by the advancing scaly edge and central clearing, and by response to antifungal rather than emollient/steroid.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the child is confirmed afebrile and systemically well with no cellulitis/impetigo features, the correct posture is a benign fungal infection managed with topical antifungal and safety-netting — not antibiotics and not urgent in-person escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic annular dermatophytosis with self-inflicted excoriation from intense pruritus; benign and self-limiting with topical antifungal therapy in a systemically well child.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Confirmation is ultimately by skin scraping (KOH microscopy / fungal culture), which telehealth cannot obtain; empirical topical antifungal treatment of a classic lesion is appropriate without it.

red_flags.description: Features of secondary bacterial infection — a spreading, hot, tender, indurated area; thick yellow or honey-coloured crusting; increasing pain, swelling or frank pus
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate cellulitis or impetiginisation superimposed on the excoriated skin, requiring antibiotics and same-day in-person review rather than topical antifungal alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated spreading bacterial skin infection in a child can progress to systemic infection.

red_flags.description: Systemic illness — fever (>38.0 C), lethargy, or a child who is unwell in himself with rapidly spreading painful redness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features would shift this from a benign superficial infection to a potentially serious soft-tissue/systemic infection needing urgent emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early sepsis or invasive soft-tissue infection.

red_flags.description: Extensive disease, scalp/hairline involvement, nail involvement, or failure to respond to a topical antifungal after 2-4 weeks
red_flags.clinical_significance: Scalp (tinea capitis), nail, or extensive/refractory dermatophytosis needs a systemic (oral) antifungal prescribed and monitored by a clinician, not topical treatment alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-treated tinea capitis can cause scarring alopecia; refractory disease persists and spreads.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the carer's fear of a 'flesh-eating'/spreading infection and the visible bleeding, and treating this as cellulitis with urgent in-person escalation and oral antibiotics when the child is afebrile, systemically well, and the bleeding is mechanical from scratching.

Prescribing a topical corticosteroid alone, or continuing it beyond a short adjunct course, which suppresses the inflammatory signs while the dermatophyte spreads — worsening and disguising the infection.
Either escalating unnecessarily to satisfy an anxious parent, or the opposite — dismissing the rash without a safety-net for evolving secondary bacterial infection.
Insisting on skin scraping/culture or a bacterial swab before starting treatment of a classic annular lesion, delaying simple effective topical antifungal therapy.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the lesion edges for warmth, induration or fluctuance (to exclude cellulitis/abscess)
Skin scraping for KOH microscopy and fungal culture to confirm the dermatophyte
A clinician's close dermatological examination of the full extent, scalp and nails
An accurate measured temperature and the other vital signs
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the classic annular tinea pattern from the carer's description and video, advise a topical antifungal with nail-cutting/anti-scratch and hygiene measures, and safety-net explicitly for secondary bacterial infection and systemic illness. Keep triage LOW (routine); do not escalate this systemically-well child to urgent in-person care, ED or 000.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis — the morphology is classic. The real risks are (a) UNDER-recognition of, or failure to safety-net for, a secondary bacterial infection, and (b) OVER-action — escalating a benign fungal infection to urgent care or prescribing antibiotics/steroid-monotherapy inappropriately.
